items.q: Which of the following is a fundamental particle: proton, electron, or neutron?
items.correct: Only the electron — protons and neutrons are made of quarks.
items.misconception: All three (proton, electron, neutron) are fundamental particles that cannot be broken down.
items.random_wrong: Only the proton is fundamental; electrons and neutrons are composite.
items.why: The atomic model is introduced with proton/neutron/electron as building blocks, so students don't update when quarks appear in later courses.

items.q: What does gravitational field strength at a point represent?
items.correct: The force per unit mass that would act on a test mass placed at that point.
items.misconception: The total gravitational force at that point regardless of mass.
items.random_wrong: The mass of the object creating the field.
items.why: Students forget the 'per unit mass' (or per unit charge) part of field strength definitions, conflating the field with the resulting force.

items.q: What is the difference between nuclear fission and fusion?
items.correct: Fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller ones; fusion joins light nuclei into a heavier one.
items.misconception: Fission and fusion are the same process, just at different scales.
items.random_wrong: Fission combines small atoms into bigger ones, and fusion splits them.
items.why: The words sound similar (both start 'fi-'), and students mix up which one combines and which one splits.

items.q: What is the main advantage of an electromagnet over a normal magnet?
items.correct: It can be switched on and off
items.why: Correct answer: It can be switched on and off. “It's cheaper” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which would NOT make an electromagnet stronger?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Plastic core. “More turns of wire” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An electromagnet with 100 turns has a certain strength. How many turns might double it (roughly)?
items.why: The real figure is 200. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
